content.title: World On
content.summary: World On was a cross-platform social adventure app designed to bring MMO-style mission systems into the real world. Users could discover location-based experiences, complete them in physical spaces, and receive digital rewards. The project reached a working MVP and closed beta on Android.
content.role_and_scope: I led most of the development work for this iteration of the product, including implementation, planning, documentation, and day-to-day execution. There were occasional collaborators, including graphic designers and other contributors, but I was the main person responsible for turning the concept into a working MVP.
content.product_context:
The product aimed to get people out of their homes and into real-world exploration, fun, and social connection. The core user flow was:

- discover nearby experiences through a location-based feed
- review photos, descriptions, tags, and steps
- complete the activity in the real world
- receive experience points and any creator-defined reward
- build a profile through participation and creation

User-generated content was central to the product. Creators defined the concept, description, steps, tags, photos, difficulty, and rewards for each experience. The longer-term vision included augmented reality-inspired ideas, but the MVP stayed simpler.
content.implementation:
The product combined geolocation-driven discovery with user-generated content and reward mechanics. Key implementation areas included:

- location-based experience discovery and feed logic
- profile and social-style app structure
- experience creation flows with descriptions, steps, tags, and photos
- reward and progression mechanics
- Firebase-backed backend behavior and app architecture

The beta ran on Android and reached Play Store distribution in closed beta. The iOS version was started but never completed.
outcomes: Turned a broad product idea into a functioning MVP.
Released the Android app to a closed beta through the Play Store.
Supported small-scale real user testing with a few dozen users.
Iterated on UX/UI from development feedback.
Did not progress to a finished iOS release or large-scale adoption.
content.security_and_quality:
- Authentication was handled through Firebase Auth.
- The app involved location-based discovery, which made privacy and data handling relevant from a practical standpoint.
- User-generated content introduced moderation and abuse-risk considerations.
- Creator-defined rewards created trust and abuse concerns that would matter more at scale.

This was not a project built around advanced security architecture, but it already involved practical concerns around auth, permissions, privacy, and user behavior controls.
lessons: Building from scratch forces clarity in product structure and technical priorities.
Strong execution matters more than endless ideation.
Geolocation and user-generated content create strong product possibilities, but they also add complexity quickly.
Getting something into users' hands matters more than repeatedly redesigning the MVP in meetings.
The project reinforced the importance of turning ambitious ideas into concrete systems that real users can try.
